div.p: Trade-offs: Speed vs Accuracy vs Hardware Requirements

Production Profiling: Use Frame Pointer (Method 1) with -fno-omit-frame-pointer. Best balance of speed and reliability. Deep Debugging: Use DWARF (Method 2) when you need inlined functions, source lines, or cannot recompile. Ultra-Low Overhead: Use LBR (Method 3) for hot-path profiling on Intel systems where 16-32 frame depth is sufficient. Hybrid Approach: Start with frame pointer for speed, fall back to DWARF for truncated stacks.
• ~200-500ns per frame • Simple implementation • No debug info needed • Async-signal-safe • Works in production
• Requires -fno-omit-frame-pointer • Loses 1 register (rbp) • ~2-5% performance hit • Breaks with optimizations • Leaf functions may omit FP

g.text: 1. Find CFI entry for current RIP

div.p: • Works with any optimization • Handles inline functions • No frame pointer needed • Shows source lines • Standard ELF section
• ~5-20μs per frame (10-50× slower) • Complex parsing • Large binary size (+10-30%) • May not be async-signal-safe • Memory allocation risk
• ~0ns unwind time • No code modification • Works on stripped binaries • Kernel-mode support • Perfect for hot-path profiling
• Intel only (no AMD equivalent) • Depth limited to 16-32 • Requires privileged access • Only captures branches, not full stack • Lost on context switch
